coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots derived from tangerine peel on emetic and diarrheal type of Bacillus cereus of packaged tofu
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Yeon Ho
Khan, Ajahar
Ahn, Ji Min
Lee, Ji Hyun
Yoon, Ki Sun
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Tofu is one of the main sources of plant-based protein in Korea; however, because of the high initial counts of Bacillus cereus, its average shelf life under refrigeration is restricted to just seven days. To prolong the shelf life of tofu, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as antimicrobial agents, combined with preservative water, to target both emetic and diarrheal strains of B. cereus. CQDs were prepared from tangerine peel using a one-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs were monodispersed quasi-spherical forms in the range of 3.6–13.5 nm with an average diameter of around 8.2 nm. The CQDs showed strong antibacterial activity against diarrheal and emetic Bacillus cereus with MIC/MBC values of 1400/1600 and 1800/2400 μg/mL, respectively. When CQDs were added to tofu preservation water for tofu packaging (2400 μg/mL), a significant effect was found in inhibiting the growth of both two diarrheal and emetic types of B. cereus compared to the regular immersion water. Additionally, the D-value of diarrheal B. cereus in the control and CQDs-added samples decreased from 90.45 h to 54.6 h at 4 °C. The population of diarrheal bacteria in tofu soaked in CQDs diminished, while growth was observed in the control sample at 15 and 20 °C. The lag time for emetic bacteria in the CQDs samples was extended compared to the control, from 17.9 h to 33.4 h and from 8.9 h to 15.3 h at 15 and 20 °C, respectively. Therefore, using CQDs-added water in packaged tofu can not only prevent the risk caused by B. cereus but also maintain the quality of tofu and extend its shelf life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus cereus
Diarrheal and emetic type
Tofu
Carbon quantum dots
Antimicrobial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of diarrheal and emetic Bacillus cereus
Initial microbial contamination levels of tofu
Effect of CQDs on the growth of B. cereus in tofu
pH, color, and texture of tofu
The effect of CQDs on the growth and survival of B. cereus in tofu
The effect of preserved water with CQDs on the quality of tofu

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from Kyung Hee University (KHU-20233671).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the preparation and application of carbon quantum dots as a preservative for packaged tofu.
(a) TEM images and (b) size distribution of the CQDs.
(a) UV–vis, emission and excitation fluorescent spectra (inset: CQD solution under daylight and UV light), and (b) FL emission spectra of CQDs under 280–500 nm of excitation wavelengths.
Antibacterial activity of carbon quantum dots against the diarrheal and emetic type of Bacillus cereus.
Growth of diarrheal and emetic type of B. cereus in the tofu immersed in water and CQDs-added water at different temperatures.
Growth and survival parameters of B. cereus on tofu soaked in water with and without CQD at various temperatures.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: tlag: lag time of growth (h), μ: specific growth rate (log CFU/g.h), Nmax: maximum population density (log CFU/g), D: decimal reduction time (h), and p: shape factor of the regression curve.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The changes in surface color, pH, and hardness of tofu stored in water with or without CQDs at 4 °C for 7 days.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant different (p < 0.05) by Duncan’s multiple range test.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots derived from tangerine peel on emetic and diarrheal type of Bacillus cereus of packaged tofu

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul, 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tofu is one of the main sources of plant-based protein in Korea; however, because of the high initial counts of Bacillus cereus, its average shelf life under refrigeration is restricted to just seven days. To prolong the shelf life of tofu, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used as antimicrobial agents, combined with preservative water, to target both emetic and diarrheal strains of B. cereus. CQDs were prepared from tangerine peel using a one-step hydrothermal method. The CQDs were monodispersed quasi-spherical forms in the range of 3.6–13.5 nm with an average diameter of around 8.2 nm. The CQDs showed strong antibacterial activity against diarrheal and emetic Bacillus cereus with MIC/MBC values of 1400/1600 and 1800/2400 μg/mL, respectively. When CQDs were added to tofu preservation water for tofu packaging (2400 μg/mL), a significant effect was found in inhibiting the growth of both two diarrheal and emetic types of B. cereus compared to the regular immersion water. Additionally, the D-value of diarrheal B. cereus in the control and CQDs-added samples decreased from 90.45 h to 54.6 h at 4 °C. The population of diarrheal bacteria in tofu soaked in CQDs diminished, while growth was observed in the control sample at 15 and 20 °C. The lag time for emetic bacteria in the CQDs samples was extended compared to the control, from 17.9 h to 33.4 h and from 8.9 h to 15.3 h at 15 and 20 °C, respectively. Therefore, using CQDs-added water in packaged tofu can not only prevent the risk caused by B. cereus but also maintain the quality of tofu and extend its shelf life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tofu, the most popular soybean product, is low in calories since it contains high-quality protein and a large amount of minerals such as iron, magnesium, and calcium (Préstamo & Fontecha, 2007). Consumption of tofu has been increasing recently due to increased interest in vegetarianism, the health benefits of tofu, and growing interest in plant-based diets (Zheng, Regenstein, Teng, & Li, 2020). Fresh tofu is one of the most popular foods in Korea, and commercially sold tofu is packaged in preservation water to prevent it from breaking or spoiling during distribution and sale. Due to its high protein content, moisture content, and neutral pH, it is a food at high risk of microbial contamination (Lee, Kwon, Chai, & Oh, 2017). In addition, tofu is prone to microorganisms growing even under refrigerated storage conditions, so it has a shorter shelf life than other soy-based products (Dotsom, Frank, & Cavaletto, 1977). Even though domestic food companies keep the distribution temperature of tofu below 10 °C using a cold chain in accordance with the Food Sanitation Act, they need technology to prolong the distribution period of tofu. Previous studies on the antimicrobial activity of tofu have directly incorporated various antimicrobial agents, such as chitosan, liposomes, and thiamine dilauryl sulfate, into tofu (Cui, Zhao, & Lin, 2015; Koo, Kwon, & Oh, 2018; No, Park, Lee, Hwang, & Meyers, 2002). However, these technologies have not been implemented in the food industry due to their high costs, complex synthetic methods, and impacts on sensory preferences (e.g., odor, color, flavor). Therefore, this study suggests that one of the challenges in tofu technology is maintaining antimicrobial functionality in water. The alternative preservation water for preservation for shape of tofu to antimicrobial based solution in the tofu package is the first study, therefore, this trial has a high novelty in the food industry and academy fields.

While various foodborne illnesses can occur from tofu, Bacillus cereus, which can change its spores in stressful conditions, has consistently been recognized as the main food poisoning pathogen (Han, Beumer, Rombouts, & Nout, 2001). Bacillus cereus produces toxins that can cause two types of food poisoning: emetic and diarrhea. The emetic type of B. cereus causes food poisoning by consuming food containing preformed heat-stable toxins, while the diarrhea type of B. cereus disease is caused by the production of toxins in the gastrointestinal tract (Johnson, Nelson, & Busta, 1982). The representative toxins of diarrheal type B. cereus are hemolytic BL (HBL) and non-hemolytic enterotoxin (NHE), while emetic type B. cereus is caused by a toxin called cereulide (Kovac et al., 2016). The emetic strain of B. cereus exhibited a higher risk than the diarrheal strain due to its excellent survival ability at 4 °C. The border temperature was found to be 10 °C, above which the emetic strain developed faster than the diarrheal strain and showed faster growth and higher heat tolerance (Kwon, Lee, & Yoon, 2019). In Korea, 31 cases of food poisoning caused by B. cereus have been reported over the past five years (Safety), 2024). A previous study found that B. cereus was found in 11 of 85 tofu samples purchased from retail markets, and the average initial contamination level of diarrheal B. cereus was estimated to be 1.84 log CFU/g. The risk of B. cereus diarrhea caused by packaged tofu in the retail market was 1.0 × 10−4 per person per day, and the main factors were storage temperature and period (Kwon, Rhee, & Yoon, 2020). Therefore, technology must be developed to reduce the risk caused by B. cereus during the distribution of tofu and extend its shelf life. Meanwhile, tofu sold commercially is generally packaged by soaking it in water in a square plastic container with a plastic film cover. Therefore, if a safe and effective antibacterial agent is added to the tofu soaking water, it is expected to reduce the risk of B. cereus and extend the shelf life of packaged tofu.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are newly introduced zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with a size of 10 nm or less and can be easily synthesized using various biomass resources such as agricultural waste and microorganisms (Chu, Lee, Chang, & Liu, 2019). The function of CQDs depends on the type of atoms connected to the carbon structure, depending on the source (Ezati, Khan, Rhim, Kim, & Molaei, 2023). Hydrothermal carbonization, one of the bottom-up synthesis methods, has the advantage of producing CQDs under conditions such as high pressure and high temperature (180–250 °C) (Ezati, Priyadarshi, & Rhim, 2022). CQDs exert antibacterial effects through various mechanisms, including physical damage to bacterial structures, oxidative damage, and photothermal effects (Zhang, He, Xiong, Mu, Deng, & Zhao, 2023). Because CQDs are very small, they can more easily penetrate inside bacteria and exert antibacterial effects (Yu et al., 2023). Due to their hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, CQDs are widely utilized in various biological applications (Kurian & Paul, 2021). Additionally, CQDs with UV-blocking, antibacterial, antioxidant, and antifungal properties can be used for food packaging and preservation purposes (Shi et al., 2019). It has been reported that CQDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) active food packaging films extended the shelf life of bananas, jujubes, and fried meatballs (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022) and chitosan/gelatin films with CQDs used for minced meat packaging effectively retarded bacterial growth and retained meat color even after storage for 24 h at 20 °C (Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). Tangerine peel, one of the raw materials for CQDs synthesis, is a biowaste residue generated globally and is one of the most underutilized natural resources. Also, tangerine peel is rich in polyphenols that have antibacterial and antioxidant activities (Shah et al., 2024). Therefore, manufacturing CQDs using these biological waste resources and using them in the food industry is a way to achieve sustainable development through resource recycling (Ezati et al., 2023).

This study is anticipated to have social impacts, including i) sustainable resource recycling by synthesizing CQDs from biological waste derived from tangerine peels, ii) straightforward application of CQDs in the food industry through an affordable and convenient synthesis method, and iii) the development of technology to control foodborne bacteria in tofu products. Therefore, the objective of this study is to prevent contamination by B. cereus and extend the shelf life by adding functional CQDs to the soaking water of packaged tofu. For this purpose, CQDs were synthesized and characterized using citrus peels. The antibacterial activity against emetic and diarrhea-type B. cereus was compared, and the effect of CQDs preservation water on the prevention of B. cereus outbreaks and the preservation of tofu quality during storage were evaluated (see Fig. 1 ).

Tangerines were purchased from a local market in Seoul, and CQDs were produced using the tangerine peel. The peel makes up 40–50 % of the fruit and contains 250 % H2O, 8.55 % protein, 2.97 % lipids, 12.8 % crude fiber, 2.881 % ash, and 18.03 % carbohydrates per dry matter (El Barnossi, Moussaid, & Housseini, 2021). The emetic (NCCP 14796) and diarrheal types (ATCC 11778) of Bacillus cereus were obtained from the Korean Culture Center of Microorganisms (KCCM, Seoul, Korea). Fresh packaged tofu [300 g tofu with 200 mL water in a polypropylene tub (10 × 10 × 4.5 cm) with polyethylene cover film] was purchased from a local market (Seoul, Korea). Penicillin was gained from Gibco (ThermoFisher Science, Waltham, MA, USA).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermally carbonizing method (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024). Typically, 1 g of dried and ground tangerine peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water with stirring, transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL), and processed in a muffle furnace at 190 °C for 7 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate the discrete particles. The aqueous part was filtered through a membrane filter of 25 mm diameter and pore size 0.20 μm (Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, England) to remove undissolved particles. The CQDs-solutions were diluted with distilled water to a concentration of 5.0 mg/mL and refrigerated at 4 °C for further analysis.

The fluorescence emission response with different excitation wavelengths for the synthesized CQDs was examined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100 FL, Hitachi, Japan) at 25 °C. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity was determined by shifting the excitation wavelength (280 nm–500 nm) at a fixed scan rate of 1200 nm/min with a slit width of 5 nm.
The optical absorption spectra of CQDs were evaluated via a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV/Vis, Mecasys Optizen POP series, Seoul, Korea) in the range 250–700 nm at 25 °C.

The size and shape distribution of the synthesized CQDs were evaluated by recording TEM micrographs using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100F, TEM, Jeol Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The average particle size and distribution of CQDs were measured using ImageJ software (Wayne Rasband and Contributors' ImageJ 1.53k, National Institute of Health, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs were analyzed using an FT-IR spectrometer (TENSOR 37 spectrophotometer with OPUS 6.0 software, Billerica, MA, USA) under the same conditions and an average resolution of 32 scans at 4 cm−1 from 4000 to 400 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of diarrheal and emetic Bacillus cereus
ce:section.ce:para: Diarrheal and emetic B. cereus strains were stored in TSB containing 20 % glycerol in a −80 °C freezer. For antibacterial testing (MIC/MBC test and time-kill assay) and tofu application, 10 μL of the stored cells were inoculated into 10 mL of sterile TSB and incubated in a rotary shaker (VS-8480SR, Vision, Seoul, Korea) at 140 rpm and 36 °C for 24 h. Activated B. cereus was diluted using the serial dilution method.

The antibacterial activity of CQDs against two types of B. cereus was evaluated by determining the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) and the time-kill assay according to the method of (Kim, Yoon, Lee, Park, & Rhim, 2022). 50 μL of the prepared B. cereus culture (5–6 log CFU/mL) was inoculated into 50 μL of the CQDs-solution so that the final concentrations were 0 (control), 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800. Add 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000, 2200, 2400, 2600, 2800, 3000 μg/mL and 100 μL of tryptic soy broth (TSB, KisanBio., Ltd., SEOUL, Korea) to a 96-well plate. Then, the 96-well plates were incubated at 36 °C for 24 h. MIC and MBC results were measured as optical density (OD) at 580 nm using Muliskan GO (Thermo Scientific, MA, USA). To test the MBC of CQDs, after incubation, all solutions from each well were inoculated onto nutrient agar (NA, KisanBio., Ltd., Seoul, Korea) and incubated at 36 °C for 24 h. To determine MBC, colonies of B. cereus were counted. The lowest concentration of CQDs equal to the OD value compared to the control was determined by MIC. The lowest concentration of CQDs at which no colonies were visible in each NA was determined by MBC. The penicillin was used for positive control.

For the time-kill assay of CQDs against B. cereus, 2 mL of diluted each pre-incubated culture (5–6 log CFU/mL) were inoculated into a sterilized Erlenmeyer flask containing 16 mL of TSB with 2 mL of each CQDs-solution of 0 (control), 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000, 2200, 2400, 2600, 2800, and 3000 μg/mL, and incubated at 36 °C and 140 rpm for 24 h. After inoculation, each solution diluted using serial dilution was inoculated into NA at each time interval (0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 h) and incubated at 36 °C for 24 h. Finally, the behavior of B. cereus treated with CQDs was determined by the change in the number of B. cereus colonies on NA at each time interval.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Initial microbial contamination levels of tofu
ce:section.ce:para: First, the initial level of microbial contamination of tofu, such as total aerobic bacteria, coliforms, Escherichia coli, yeast and mold, and Bacillus cereus contained in tofu and preserved water, was determined according to the method of the Korea Food Code (Code, 2024). For this, 25 g of tofu was homogenized using a Stomacher (Bagmixer 400, Interscience, Saint Nom, France) with 225 mL of sterilized peptone water. Then, 1 mL of each sample, such as the mixed solution and preserved water contained in the tofu package, was inoculated onto 3M Petri films of AC, EC, and YM (3M Co., Ltd., MN, USA) for aerobic bacteria, coliforms and Escherichia coli, and yeast and mold, respectively and NA and mannitol egg yolk polymyxin agar (MYP, KisanBio, Seoul Korea) for B. cereus. These plates were incubated at 36 °C for 24 h, and viable colonies were counted in each medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on the growth of B. cereus in tofu
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of tofu was cut aseptically and inoculated with 50 μL of pre-cultured emetic and diarrheal types of B. cereus, then immersed in 9 mL of each preservation water, including control water and 2400 μg/mL of CQDs solution, and stored at different temperatures of 4, 10, 15, and 20 °C. At the predetermined time interval, the tofu and preserved water were homogenized with 45 mL of sterilized peptone water using a stomacher for 2 min. Then, 200 μL of solution was inoculated into NA and incubated at 36 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the colonies on the cultured NA plates were counted, and the growth and survival curves were fitted.

To determine the percentage of cells damaged by CQDs-preserve water, 200 μL of the solution stored at 4 °C for 24 h was inoculated onto NA and MYP and incubated at 36 °C for 24 h. The percentage of damaged cells was then calculated by comparing the number of viable colonies of B. cereus in NA and MYP as follows:
(1)
Cells
damage
( % )
=
N 1
/
N 0
× 100
N0 represents the number of colonies on the NA medium after culturing, whereas N1 indicates the number of colonies on the MYP medium after culturing.

Growth and survival parameters of B. cereus were calculated using the modified Gompertz equation (Gibson, Bratchell, & Roberts, 1987) and the modified Weibull equation (Geeraerd, Valdramidis, & Van Impe, 2005), respectively, using GraphPad Prism 7.0 software (San Diego, CA, USA). The modified Gompertz equation is as follows:
(2)
N t
=
N 0
+ C
exp
(
− exp
(
(
2.718 μ / Δ N
)
×
(
t lag
− t
)
+ 1
)
)
where N0 and Nt are the log number of bacteria (log CFU/g) of initial and at time t, respectively, ΔN is the difference between the number of the initial and final bacterial count, L is the lag time before growth (h), and μ is a specific growth rate (log CFU/h.g).

The modified Weibull equation is as follows:
(3)
Log
N t
= Log
N 0
–
(
t / D
)
p
where N0 is the initial number of bacteria, D is the decimal reduction time of the bacteria, t is time (h), and p is the shape factor of the curve, indicating the deviation from normal growth of microorganisms (p = 1). For p < 1, the shape of the Weibull model corresponds to a concave upward curve, and for p > 1, it corresponds to a convex downward curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH, color, and texture of tofu
ce:section.ce:para: pH changes during tofu storage were evaluated. For this, 5 g of each sample was homogenized with 45 mL of distilled water using a stocker for 2 min, and then the pH value of each sample was measured using an Orion 3-star pH benchtop (Thermo Scientific, MA, USA).

The color change of CQDs-treated tofu was evaluated by measuring L∗ (brightness), a∗ (green/redness), and b∗ (blue/yellow) color values using a colorimeter (Minolta CR-400, Osaka, Japan) with the background of a standard white color plate (L∗ = 97.75, a∗ = −0.49, and b∗ = 1.96).

The hardness of the tofu was measured to evaluate changes in the texture of the tofu during storage. For this, the tofu was cut into small pieces (30 × 10 × 10 mm), and the hardness of the tofu was measured using a texture analyzer (CT-10K, Brookfield, Middleboro, MA, USA). A blade-shaped TA9 probe was used in texture profile analysis (TPA) mode with a speed of 0.5 mm/s, a final distance of 8.0 mm, and a load of 67 mN.

All tests were analyzed at least three times, and results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, and the significant difference (p < 0.05) between each mean value was evaluated through Duncan’s Multiple Range Test using the SPSS statistical analysis computer program (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The microstructure of the CQDs was observed using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 a. The CQDs showed a quasi-spherical shape of particles in the range of 3.6–13.5 nm with an average diameter of around 8.2 nm. The size distribution of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2b using a Gaussian distribution model (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2024). TEM results showed that the CQDs were monodisperse with sizes in the quantum range (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2024).

The chemical structure of the CQDs was evaluated via FT-IR analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . The peak around 3271 cm−1, stretching vibration peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1769 cm−1 were due to the O-H/N-H groups, stretching of C=C bonds polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and carbonyl groups (C=O bond), respectively (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). The stretching vibrations of methyl and methylene groups were confirmed by the presence of IR peaks around 2918 and 2872 cm−1. Additionally, the asymmetric and symmetric vibrational modes of C-N (amide group), O-H (phenolic hydroxyl group), and C-O-C bond can be indicated by the occurrence of IR peaks around 1350, 1237, and 1061 cm−1, respectively (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). FTIR results confirmed that the surface of CQDs was composed of -COOH, N-H, -OH, CO, and C=C groups, making CQDs water-soluble with proving functional properties.

As shown in the inset photo in Fig. 4 a, the CQDs-solution appeared brown under daylight but showed blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light, indirectly indicating that CQDs were formed through the hydrothermal process. It is known that CQDs emit fluorescence due to the quantum confinement effect when exposed to ultraviolet light (Fatahi, Esfandiari, & Ranjbar, 2020; Khan, Ezati, et al., 2024). The UV–visible spectra (Fig. 4a) show that the CQDs solution exhibited a sharp characteristic absorbance peak of CQDs centered around 292 nm due to π-π∗ and n-π∗ transition of the conjugated C=C bonds and a carboxyl group (C=O bonds) (Ding, Wei, & Xiong, 2014; Fatahi et al., 2020).

Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs had a pronounced wavelength dependence and shifted to a longer region as the excitation wavelength increased from 280 nm to 500 nm. At the optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, CQDs exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 439 nm. In general, it is known that CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent tunable fluorescence responses due to their various sizes and shapes (Fatahi et al., 2020; Khan, Ezati, et al., 2024).

The antibacterial activity of CQDs against diarrheal and emetic types of B. cereus was investigated using MIC/MBC values (data not shown). The MIC/MBC values of penicillin as positive control were 0.1/0.1 μg/mL and 0.1/0.125 μg/mL against diarrheal and emetic strains of B. cereus, respectively. In previous study, the MIC of penicillin against various strains of B. cerues was observed in the range of 0.5–64 μg/mL (Turnbull et al., 2004). The MIC/MBC values of CQDs-treated diarrheal and emetic types of B. cereus were 1400/1600 and 1800/2400 μg/mL, respectively. The degree of resistance of B. cereus to CQDs was different depending on the strain type, with emetic strains appearing to have stronger resistance than the diarrheal strain. The citrus peel extract treated with methanol for 72 h did not show any MIC/MBC against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli up to 4000 μg/mL (Ghoreishi & Mortazavi-Derazkola, 2025). Additionally, the MIC of the acid-hydrolyzed citrus peel extract was 20 mg/mL against B. cereus (Min, Kim, Lee, Kim, & Paik, 2014). The antibacterial activity of the citrus peel extract, as determined by the diffusion assay, demonstrated the least effect against B. cereus, Listeria monocytogenes, E. coli, and Salmonella senftenberg compared to other citrus peel extracts such as orange, lemon, and grapefruit (Shehata, Awad, Asker, El Sohaimy, Abd El-Aziz, & Youssef, 2021).

Fig. 5 shows the results of the time-kill analysis of CQDs against diarrheal and emetic strains. A dramatic change in the number of each type of B. cereus was observed in the CQDs-added groups, destroying all cells of the diarrheal and emetic strains within 9 and 12 h, respectively, under CQDs treatment conditions. The total colony count test result also showed that emetic strains have higher resistance to CQDs than diarrheal strains, which was consistent with the MIC/MBC test results.

When B. cereus is exposed to stress conditions, the vegetative cells of B. cereus can change into spore form to resist the stress (Fermanian et al., 1993). It is known that the decimal reduction time (D-value), which kills 90 % of bacteria at a specific temperature, was higher for the emetic strain of B. cereus than for the diarrheal strain, which means that the emetic strain has stronger stress resistance than the diarrheal strain (Carroll et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 1982; Kwon et al., 2019) performed whole-genome profiling of diarrheal (n = 3) and emetic (n = 30) B. cereus isolates from food and human foodborne outbreaks and found that 33 strains of B. cereus could be classified into three different groups based on their chromosomal genetic profiles. Although the exact mechanism of how emetic strains have stronger stress resistance than diarrheal strains has not yet been revealed, this phenomenon is presumed to occur because the genetic makeup of emetic and diarrheal strains is different. Additionally, although many previous studies have shown differences in stress resistance between diarrheal and emetic B. cereus, the exact structure of diarrheal and emetic B. cereus spores has not yet been determined. In general, the antibacterial activity of CQDs is influenced by the source of CQDs, synthesis method, and particle size (Nishshankage, Fernandez, Pallewatta, Buddhinie, & Vithanage, 2024). Although the exact mechanism for the antibacterial activity of CQDs is not clearly elucidated, many researchers infer ionic interactions between CQDs and bacterial cell walls. Gram-positive bacteria have a single thick cell wall that contains a layer of peptidoglycan, and they have a relatively positive charge compared to Gram-negative bacteria (Khan, Ezati, et al., 2024; Sul et al., 2023). Additionally, another suggestion for the antibacterial activity mechanism of CQDs is that the oxygen molecules in CQDs induce reactive oxygen species (ROS), which cause cell wall lysis (Khan, Ezati, Kim, & Rhim, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CQDs on the growth and survival of B. cereus in tofu
ce:section.ce:para: First, through an initial contamination test, it was confirmed that total aerobic bacteria, coliforms, E. coli, yeast and mold were not present in the commercial tofu used in this study (data not shown).

Fig. 6 shows the growth of two types of B. cereus on tofu soaked in water and CQDs-solutions at 4, 10, 15, and 20 °C. The growth and survival kinetic parameters of both B. cereus types are summarized in Table 1 . In general, growth of both B. cereus types was observed at 15 °C and 20 °C, but growth did not occur at low temperatures of 4 °C and 10 °C. These results suggest that tofu needs to be stored at low temperatures during distribution. On the other hand, CQDs-added water inhibited the growth of both B. cereus types in tofu compared to water, and the effect also affected the type of B. cereus and storage temperature. In tofu immersed in CQDs-added water, diarrheal B. cereus had a lower growth rate or faster death rate than emetic B. cereus at all storage temperatures. These results are mainly attributed to the antibacterial activity of CQDs and are in good agreement with the antimicrobial test results of CQDs (MIC/MBC results and Fig. 5). Interestingly, in tofu with CQDs-added water, both B. cereus types were growth inhibited and destroyed at low storage temperatures (4 °C, 10 °C), whereas, at high storage temperatures (15 °C, 20 °C), the antibacterial effect decreased with increasing temperature and showed only a bacteriostatic effect. This result also suggests that low-temperature distribution is more effective in increasing the tofu preservation effect of using CQDs-added water.

Table 1 summarizes the growth and survival parameters of both B. cereus types on the tofu preserved in water and CQDs-added water at different temperatures determined using eqns. (1) and (2). Typically, the temperature of an open refrigerator in a supermarket or convenience store is estimated to be around 15 °C, which is considered a serious temperature abuse for refrigerated food. Since packaged tofu sold commercially is sold in an open refrigerator, it is necessary to evaluate the antibacterial risk of both B. cereus types in tofu. The microbial risk of emetic B. cereus in tofu preserved in CQDs-added water decreased by extending the lag time of growth (tlag) (33.4 h) with lower maximum population density (Nmax) (6.84 log CFU/g) compared with those immersed in water (17.9 h and 8.20 log CFU/g, respectively) at 15 °C. Diarrheal B. cereus contained in tofu showed different growth behavior patterns depending on whether CQDs were added to the immersion water. When only immersing water was used, the tlag, μ (specific growth rate), and Nmax of diarrheal strains were 27.3h, 0.03 log CFU/h, and 5.77 log CFU/g, respectively, at 15 °C. However, the decimal reduction time (D-value) of diarrheal B. cereus in tofu preserved in CQDs-added water was 50.21 h at 15 °C. Generally, 20 °C is used as an accelerated test temperature for refrigerated foods (Corradini & Peleg, 2007), and the behavior patterns of all samples at 20 °C were similar to those at 15 °C. The tlag of emetic B. cereus in the tofu was extended approximately two-fold when CQDs were used in the preservative water. Growth of the emetic strain at 20 °C had an Nmax of 8.00 log CFU/g for the control sample and 7.00 log CFU/g for the tofu immersed in CQDs-added water.

D-values for diarrheal B. cereus in tofu immersed in water and CQDs-added water were 90.45 and 54.36 h, respectively, while emetic B. cereus in tofu immersed in CQDs-added water was 46.07 h, and the behavior of emetic B. cereus in tofu immersed in water showed the stationary growth at 4 °C. The D-values of the diarrheal and emetic B. cereus in tofu preserved with CQDs-added water at 10 °C were 20.35 and 38.72 h, respectively. The population of diarrheal B. cereus in tofu of the control sample at 10 °C slowly decreased, while the growth of emetic type in tofu preserved water without CQDs was not detected at 10 °C. A similar phenomenon was also reported in a previous study, demonstrating that emetic B. cereus cells showed survival behavior below 9 °C and a growth tendency above 11 °C, but no growth or survival of emetic B. cereus was observed in the head at 10 °C (Kwon et al., 2019; Kwon et al., 2019) also showed similar results in the behavior patterns of two types of B. cereus in tofu at various storage temperatures. It is important to note that the results from the B. cereus tofu application test (in vivo) do not necessarily align with antimicrobial tests conducted in broth, such as the MIC/MBC test and the time-kill assay (in vitro).

CQDs from various sources are applied to numerous foods to reduce microbial risks using active packages and food additives (Riahi et al., 2024). prepared a cellulose nanofiber/pullulan composite film supplemented with CQDs prepared from avocado peel and used it for the packaging of tofu. They found that the population of E. coli and L. monocytogenes in tofu packaged with this composite film for 9 days was decreased by 83 % and 99 %, respectively. It has also been reported that coating fresh cucumbers with chitosan-encapsulated CQDs prepared from kelp powder inhibited the growth of total aerobic bacteria for 15 days (Fan, Zhang, Fan, & Jiang, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of preserved water with CQDs on the quality of tofu
ce:section.ce:para: The average shelf life of tofu stored at 10 °C is 7 days. However, in this study, food quality, including color, pH, and texture, was assessed by soaking tofu in preservation water containing CQDs for 7 days while keeping it at 4 °C. The results are shown in Table 2 . CQDs influenced the color properties of tofu. The a∗-value (greenness/redness) of tofu preserved in water showed a significant change from −1.87 ± 0.00 to −1.30 ± 0.10 over 7 days. However, in the CQDs-added water, the lightness (L∗) of the tofu significantly decreased from 87.3 ± 0.8 (0 d) to 74.4 ± 1.2 (7 d), while a∗ and b∗ values significantly increased from −1.84 ± 0.10 to 74.4 ± 1.2 (7 d). 2.80 ± 0.00 and 15.4 ± 0.3 to 16.1 ± 0.3, respectively, which was mainly due to the brown color of CQDs.

After 7 days, the pH of tofu preserved in water and CQDs-added water at 4 °C was significantly different (p < 0.05). The initial pH values of tofu preserved in water and CQDs-added water were 6.57 ± 0.03 and 6.57 ± 0.02, respectively, and changed to 6.83 ± 0.01 and 6.45 ± 0.01, respectively, after 7 days. The significant increase in the pH value of tofu preserved in water may be due to the hydrogen ion concentration of tofu being affected by the pH of distilled water. However, the change in the pH of tofu preserved with CQDs-added water was due to the acidic conditions (4.27–4.62) of various citrus peel extracts (Park & Kim, 2013).

The initial values of hardness of tofu preserved in water and CQDs-added water were 1.04 ± 0.02 and 1.04 ± 0.04 N, respectively. After 7 days, the tofu preserved in water maintained a similar level of hardness (1.07 ± 0.01 N); however, the hardness of tofu preserved in CQDs-added water increased significantly (p < 0.05) to 1.33 ± 0.01 N. The coagulation of tofu is due to the cross-linking of 7S conglycinin, a trimer such as α submit, α′ submit, and β submit, with 11S glycinin, a hexamer composed of basic and acidic polypeptides in soy milk (Ren, Tang, Zhang, & Guo, 2009; Taski-Ajdukovic, Djordjevic, Vidic, & Vujakovic, 2010; Utsumi, Maruyama, Satoh, & Adachi, 2002). It is known that when various fruit juices such as carrots and cucumbers are added to tofu, the numerous positively or negatively charged molecular ions contained in the juice act as a cross-linking agent for the tofu, increasing the hardness of the tofu (Yoon & Kim, 1997). Likewise, functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs, as seen in the FT-IR test results (Fig. 3), are believed to increase the hardness of tofu by combining with 7S conglycinin and 11S glycinin of soy protein.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from tangerine peel using a one-step hydrothermal method and used for the preservation of packaged tofu. The CQDs exhibited significant antibacterial effects against diarrheal and emetic strains of Bacillus cereus, with MIC/MBC values of 1400/1600 and 1800/2400, respectively. The emetic strain was more resistant to CQDs than the diarrheal strain. When CQDs-added water was used in tofu packaging, the growth of two types of B. cereus was suppressed significantly, and the effect was much greater when stored at low temperatures (4 °C and 10 °C) than at higher temperatures (15 °C and 20 °C). At refrigerated temperatures (4 °C and 10 °C), the delta values for both strains of B. cereus in the CQDs samples were lower than those in the control sample. Moreover, the lag time and specific growth rate of the two strains of B. cereus in the CQDs-solution increased while the maximum population density decreased at 15 °C and 20 °C compared to the control. However, the issue highlighted in this study is that when CQDs were added to the tofu preservation water (2400 μg/mL), the surface color became slightly darker, yet the quality of the tofu, including its hardness, improved. Future research is needed on various CQDs from different sources that do not change the surface color of the tofu. The concept of this study, which involves incorporating an antibacterial substance into the preservation water during tofu packaging, can serve as one of the hurdle techniques. Moreover, future research into functional preservatives targeting antibacterial, antioxidant, and tofu quality enhancement is essential. Therefore, CQDs derived from tangerine peel through upcycling, known for its convenient synthesis and low cost, could be utilized in the future food industry to preserve tofu quality and extend its shelf life compared to B. cereus in packaged tofu.

Yeon Ho Kim: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ajahar Khan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Ji Min Ahn: Data curation. Ji Hyun Lee: Data curation. Ki Sun Yoon: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from Kyung Hee University (KHU-20233671).
